summary: McKenzie argues that software businesses should write blogs aimed at their customers rather than at fellow developers or entrepreneurs. He explains the 'Pyramid of Participation' concept — most blog audiences are passive consumers, not creators — and advocates inverting it to convert passive readers into engaged customers. Using Declan Software's Japanese language learning tools as a concrete example, he demonstrates how a customer-focused blog provides genuinely useful content that builds trust, drives organic search traffic, and creates word-of-mouth referrals. The post emphasizes writing in your customers' language, having a clear goal for every post, and offering a mix of content types to sustain engagement.
key_points: Most software blogs make the mistake of writing for other developers and bloggers rather than for their actual paying customers, who are typically non-technical
The Pyramid of Participation shows that 1% of any community creates content, 10% contributes, and the rest passively consume — successful customer blogs invert this by activating passive readers
Providing genuinely useful free content builds trust and creates organic search engine traffic, since Google ranks blog content for long-tail queries surprisingly quickly
Writing in your customers' language rather than tech jargon improves both SEO (matching how customers actually search) and relatability
Every blog post should have a clear goal and call to action — whether generating a trial download, encouraging a return visit, or prompting readers to share with peers
money_quotes: Writing a blog about and for your customers, as opposed to about and for your business, is a great marketing investment.
My typical customer has no clue who Joel Spolsky is. She thinks '/.' is what happens when her kitty or 6 year old start banging away on the keyboard.

Blogging is traditionally for bloggers. And this is bad, because unless you're pitching products to the 53,651 members of the Silicon Valley echo chamber, not counting the Squidoo Lenses and other 'I can't believe its not blog' innovations.

Your customer likely does not care about the technology you are using, the difficulties of porting your code to .NET, the bug that nearly wiped out your version control system. Your customer likes to hear about themselves.
If you write stuff of use to your target audience, how-to's, things they are genuinely interested in hearing, they will beat a path to your door.
Do you know where your user base hangs out? Because if you don't, I'm worried for you.
The ads are content by then, because your blog is about things your customers like and find useful and your customers are obviously interested in what you have to sell.
key_insight: A business blog should be written for and about your customers — not your industry peers — providing genuinely useful content in their language to build trust, drive organic search traffic, and convert passive readers into buyers.
